text: Partial sleep deprivation (4 hours sleep vs 8 hours) reduces 12-minute self-paced running distance by approximately 6% in recreational runners.
Partial sleep deprivation significantly increases rating of perceived exertion during self-paced running with a large effect size, meaning the same effort feels substantially harder after poor sleep.
Partial sleep deprivation reduces running speed during self-paced exercise with a moderate effect size, indicating runners naturally slow their pace after inadequate sleep.

Partial sleep deprivation lowers heart rate, oxygen uptake (VO2), and minute ventilation during self-paced running, suggesting reduced cardiorespiratory drive rather than central cardiovascular limitation.

Blood lactate concentration and motivation are not significantly affected by partial sleep deprivation, suggesting the performance decrement is driven by increased perceived effort and altered pacing rather than metabolic capacity or conscious motivation.
Partial sleep deprivation reduces core body temperature before exercise, which may contribute to impaired endurance performance through suboptimal physiological readiness.
Partial sleep deprivation impairs mood and cognitive function (simple reaction time and attention via barrage test) both before and after exercise in runners.
